nativeRange.habitat: Temperate to subtropical demersal species occurring on sand, mud, and rubbly bottoms off beaches and within bays and sloughs, often near seagrass beds. Reported from the intertidal zone to roughly 91 m depth, though most commonly encountered between 1 and 21 m.

decorPreferences: Deep fine sand substrate (4+ inches / 10 cm)
Wide open sandy floor for foraging and burying
Rounded tank corners to reduce collision and stress
Secure lid (rays can occasionally launch out of open-top systems)

diet.notes: Feeds in the wild on benthic invertebrates and small fishes — primarily crustaceans, polychaete worms, small molluscs, and occasional teleosts. In captivity, has been reported to accept small pieces of cleaned squid, fresh marine fish, scallops, mussels, and live or frozen ghost shrimp. Newly imported specimens commonly refuse to feed initially; dimming aquarium lights and offering live feeder shrimp on the substrate has been reported to help trigger feeding responses.

goodWith: Large peaceful fish that occupy the mid and upper water column
Tankmates too large to be considered prey
avoidWith: Shrimp, small crabs, and small molluscs (will be consumed)
Small fish that may be eaten
Other bottom-dwelling fish that compete for floor space
Sea urchins and other spiny invertebrates that could injure the ray

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by MOs810 (Gdynia Aquarium), 3409x2402. Crisp, well-lit oblique full-body view of a live vibrant ray showing the round pectoral disc and fine pale-speckled/mottled dorsal pattern; most striking of the pool.
Photo by Coughdrop12, 4032x3024. Sharp top-down view clearly showing the near-circular disc, short tail and the diagnostic pale-rosette mottling; complementary dorsal angle.
Photo by michael h j c, 2048x1536. Whole animal top-down in a natural shallow tidepool showing round disc and reticulated pattern; minor surface glare over part of the disc.

summary: The Round Stingray (Urobatis halleri) is a small temperate ray native to coastal sand flats along the Eastern Pacific coast, from California to Ecuador. It is sometimes considered one of the more manageable rays for advanced aquarists, but still demands a large footprint, a cool tank, and an experienced keeper.
careNotes:
The Round Stingray is a small, comparatively hardy temperate ray that has been kept successfully more often than tropical species such as the Blue Dot Stingray, but it remains an expert-only animal due to its size at maturity, large footprint requirements, and temperature needs. As a temperate species native to the Eastern Pacific, it is generally advised to keep the aquarium cooler than a standard tropical reef tank; cited husbandry ranges cluster around the high teens to mid 20s in Celsius, and chronic exposure to typical tropical reef temperatures (around 26–28°C) has been reported to shorten lifespan.

Footprint is more important than volume. A long, wide, shallow aquarium with a deep, fine-sand bed (at least 10 cm) and wide-open swimming lanes is generally considered essential; tanks with a footprint smaller than roughly 180 cm by 60 cm are widely considered inadequate for adults. Rounded tank corners are commonly recommended to reduce collision injuries. Coarse substrates such as crushed coral have been reported to abrade the ray's ventral surface and should be avoided.

The caudal spine is venomous and is used defensively; caution is advised during tank maintenance, netting, and acclimation. Copper-based medications should never be used with rays or other elasmobranchs (the cartilaginous-skeleton group that includes sharks and rays). High dissolved oxygen and stable water parameters are generally considered important.

Most reported aggression in this species tends to be directed at prey items rather than tankmates, making the Round Stingray semi-aggressive in practice — a threat to small fish and invertebrates it can fit in its mouth, but generally tolerant of appropriately sized companions. A minimum system volume of around 700 liters is commonly recommended, prioritizing floor space over total water volume to give the ray adequate room to forage and rest.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; adult females have been reported to produce up to two litters of up to six pups per year in the wild. Captive breeding has been documented at public aquariums but is generally considered impractical for private aquariums and has not been reliably replicated outside of institutional settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Lists species under the older genus 'Urolophus sp.' but description matches Urobatis halleri.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Urotrygonidae, Myliobatiformes), native range (Eastern Pacific: northern California to Ecuador), depth (0-91 m), temperature range (17.2-28.5°C, mean 23.6°C), max total length (58 cm), and reproduction (ovoviviparous, up to 6 pups per litter, two litters per year).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank requirements (180 gal minimum, 8x4 ft preferred footprint), temperature (65-75°F), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (1.020-1.025), kH (8-12), disc width (~10 in), care level (Expert), temperament (semi-aggressive), and substrate requirements.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), copper sensitivity, and reported size to ~12 inches. Notes also that the species can occasionally jump and benefits from a secure lid.

fish.breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; captive breeding has been recorded in public aquariums but is not reliably reproducible in private aquariums.
